refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR conflates three distinct functional obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry available to downstream planning and execution functions. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design iteration.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that a worksite spatial model exists in a defined format (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature set) and can be queried by planning/execution functions to retrieve feature geometry and attributes within specified accuracy tolerances.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (e.g., structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and exclusion zones per operational policy.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate terrain, obstacle, and work-zone data into a unified spatial model accessible to planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The parent FR (CR-NGHE-006) remains composite; the sub-FRs are atomic and ready for independent DP and IM assignment.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; maintain zone-feature mappings in a relational schema; provide zone-based query API.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a voxel grid with occupancy and semantic labels; update grid incrementally as new observations arrive; convert grid to feature primitives (planes, edges, clusters) on demand for planning queries.

DP4: Ingest raw sensor streams and apply rule-based feature extraction (e.g., detect vertical surfaces as walls, horizontal surfaces as floors/obstacles); store extracted features in a feature catalog with spatial indexing; provide feature lookup and spatial range queries.

DP5: Maintain a hybrid representation: coarse topological graph of zones/regions linked to fine-grained geometric feature clouds; allow planning to query at appropriate abstraction level (region connectivity vs. detailed obstacle geometry).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Maintain a hybrid representation combining a coarse topological graph of zones/regions (linked to safety policies and operational constraints) with fine-grained geometric feature clouds (supporting detailed collision avoidance and task planning). Expose dual-level query interfaces: (a) topological queries for high-level route planning and zone access control, (b) geometric queries for low-level motion planning and hazard avoidance. Update both layers incrementally as new sensor observations arrive, with explicit consistency rules between layers.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between topological layer and safety policy enforcement could lock policy changes to topology refactoring—mitigate by parameterizing safety constraints separately from zone definitions.
Synchronization between coarse and fine-grained layers during incremental updates could introduce race conditions or stale data—mitigate by defining explicit consistency checkpoints and versioning.
Dual-layer query interface may create consumer confusion or inconsistent interpretations—mitigate by providing clear semantics documentation and validation helpers.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 avoids premature commitment to specific sensing modalities (DP1), rigid zone decomposition (DP2), or single-abstraction representations (DP3, DP4). The hybrid approach decouples high-level planning (which needs topology and policy) from low-level motion planning (which needs geometry), reducing unnecessary information flow. Coupling risks are manageable through explicit interface contracts and versioning.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite topology is relatively stable during a planning/execution cycle; Risk: Rapid dynamic changes (e.g., temporary obstacles, zone closures) may require frequent topology updates, degrading planning efficiency.

Assumption: Safety policies and operational constraints can be expressed as zone-level or feature-level attributes; Risk: Complex spatial constraints (e.g., 'no equipment within 5m of active excavation') may not map cleanly to zone or feature properties, requiring custom constraint solvers.

Assumption: Sensor data quality and coverage are sufficient to populate both topological and geometric layers; Risk: Occluded or sparse regions may leave topology incomplete or geometry ambiguous, forcing conservative planning or manual annotation.
Assumption: Planning and execution functions can tolerate eventual consistency between layers; Risk: If real-time synchronization is required, the hybrid model introduces latency and complexity.

Assumption: Feature classification (walls, obstacles, hazards) is well-defined and consistent across the worksite; Risk: Ambiguous or novel features may be misclassified, leading to unsafe or inefficient plans.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP5 by explicitly separating concerns: (1) Topological layer: directed acyclic graph of zones with edges representing allowed transitions; zone attributes include safety policies (e.g., access restrictions, speed limits) and operational metadata (e.g., resource availability). (2) Geometric layer: spatial index (e.g., octree or R-tree) of feature primitives (points, line segments, polygons, voxels) with semantic labels and confidence scores. (3) Consistency layer: versioned snapshots of both layers; explicit rules for propagating updates (e.g., new obstacle in zone X invalidates cached paths through X). (4) Query interface: separate APIs for topology (zone reachability, policy lookup) and geometry (nearest obstacle, collision check). This revision clarifies module boundaries and reduces coupling between policy, topology, and geometry.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial representation and query engine managing hybrid topological-geometric world model with dual-level interfaces for route planning and motion planning

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives sensor observations (point clouds, zone occupancy, topology updates) to incrementally update both topological and geometric layers

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Exposes topological query interface for high-level route planning and zone access control; exposes geometric query interface for low-level motion planning and hazard avoidance

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals consistency violations or constraint breaches detected between topological and geometric layers

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE (Next-Generation Hybrid Environment) is explicitly designed for multi-layer spatial representation. Hybrid topology-geometry fusion, dual-level queries, and incremental updates are core to its purpose. Minimal diffusion: only sensor input (Telemetry) and query consumers (Tasking, SafetyAlert) cross boundary.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns motion control and state, not world modeling. Placing hybrid representation here couples spatial reasoning tightly to vehicle dynamics, violates separation of concerns. Topological queries would diffuse to planning layers; geometric queries would diffuse to motion controllers.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation owns remote operator interface and command relay, not spatial representation. Hybrid world model is orthogonal to teleoperation concerns. High diffusion: both topological and geometric queries would need to escape to planning and control modules.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns constraint enforcement and alerts, not world modeling. While consistency rules between layers have safety implications, the core DP (hybrid representation and dual-level queries) is not a safety service responsibility. Geometric queries would diffuse to motion planning; topological queries would diffuse to route planning.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of this DP because it is explicitly architected as a hybrid spatial representation engine. Its core responsibility is to maintain and expose multi-layer world models (topological and geometric) with specialized query interfaces. Incremental updates from sensor telemetry and consistency management between layers are intrinsic to NGHE's design purpose. Containment is strong because all three aspects of the DP (hybrid representation, dual-level queries, incremental updates with consistency rules) are cohesive within a single spatial reasoning domain.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution is not yet implemented or is immature, the DP may need temporary staging in a more general-purpose module (e.g., Vehicle or Safety Services) until NGHE is ready. Mitigation: Ensure NGHE is on the critical path and adequately resourced.

Risk: Dual-level query interfaces (topological and geometric) may require careful API design to avoid leaking implementation details. Mitigation: Define clear query contracts and version them independently.

Risk: Consistency rules between topological and geometric layers may become complex as sensor noise and update latency increase. Mitigation: Establish explicit consistency invariants and monitoring in NGHE; escalate violations to SafetyAlert.

Risk: Incremental update logic may introduce subtle state corruption if not carefully synchronized. Mitigation: Use immutable snapshots or versioned representations within NGHE to ensure atomic transitions.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria explicitly require 'worksite model/map generated and available to planning/execution functions for agreed set of site features.' NGHE Solution's dual-level query interfaces (topological for route planning, geometric for motion planning) directly satisfy this. Verification can measure: (1) model availability latency post-sensor-update, (2) query response times for both interface types, (3) coverage of agreed site features in both layers, (4) consistency violation detection rate.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the single owner of the hybrid spatial representation DP. Interfaces are well-defined: Telemetry (inbound sensor observations), Tasking (outbound topological/geometric queries), SafetyAlert (outbound consistency violations). No ambiguity about which module owns the world model or which modules consume it. Allocation aligns with 'worksite safety plan and operational policies' constraint: topological layer explicitly links zones to safety policies; geometric layer supports hazard avoidance.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly and completely addresses FR. FR requires 'capture and represent worksite spatial features in machine-readable model.' DP5 specifies: (1) hybrid representation (topological + geometric) capturing both abstract zones/regions and fine-grained features, (2) machine-readable dual-level query interfaces, (3) incremental update mechanism for continuous sensor integration, (4) consistency rules ensuring coherence. No functional gap. DP is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Incremental update synchronization. If topological and geometric layer updates arrive asynchronously from Telemetry, race conditions or stale-state inconsistencies could occur. Mitigation: Implement versioned snapshots or transactional update semantics within NGHE to ensure atomic layer transitions. Define explicit ordering guarantees (e.g., geometric updates trigger topological reconciliation, or vice versa).

Moderate risk: Query interface leakage. Tasking consumers (route planning, motion planning) may attempt to bypass dual-level abstraction and directly access internal layer representations. Mitigation: Enforce strict API contracts; version query interfaces independently; document which queries are topological vs. geometric and their performance/consistency guarantees.

Low risk: SafetyAlert coupling. Consistency violations detected in NGHE must be signaled to Safety Services, but SafetyAlert is a separate module. If Safety Services does not respond quickly, NGHE may accumulate unresolved violations. Mitigation: Define escalation timeouts and fallback behaviors (e.g., conservative motion constraints if consistency cannot be restored).

Low risk: Sensor observation latency. If Telemetry updates arrive with high latency or out-of-order, NGHE's incremental update logic may produce stale or inconsistent representations. Mitigation: Implement timestamp-based ordering and staleness detection; expose data age to consumers via query metadata.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score justified). NGHE Solution is purpose-built for multi-layer spatial representation. All three DP components (hybrid representation, dual-level queries, incremental updates with consistency rules) are cohesive within NGHE's domain. Minimal diffusion: only sensor input (Telemetry) and query consumers (Tasking, SafetyAlert) cross the boundary. No evidence of DP logic scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Ownership is unambiguous and architecturally sound.

recommended_revisions: Clarify consistency rules between topological and geometric layers. Specify: (a) which layer is authoritative in case of conflict (e.g., geometric observations override stale topological assumptions), (b) reconciliation strategy (e.g., geometric updates trigger topological re-inference), (c) acceptable staleness windows for each layer. Document as formal invariants in NGHE design.

Define query interface versioning and backward compatibility strategy. Specify: (a) topological query schema (e.g., zone adjacency, access control predicates), (b) geometric query schema (e.g., nearest-obstacle, collision-free-path), (c) how schema evolution is managed without breaking Tasking consumers.

Establish incremental update atomicity guarantees. Specify: (a) whether updates are per-observation or batched, (b) whether topological and geometric updates are synchronized or independent, (c) how NGHE handles out-of-order or duplicate observations from Telemetry.

Add explicit acceptance criterion for consistency monitoring. Specify: (a) acceptable rate of consistency violations (e.g., <1% of updates), (b) detection latency (e.g., <100ms), (c) escalation procedure to SafetyAlert and fallback behavior if violations persist.

Document sensor observation requirements for Telemetry interface. Specify: (a) expected point cloud density and update frequency, (b) zone occupancy observation format, (c) topology update triggers (e.g., new zone detected, boundary moved), (d) timestamp and ordering guarantees.

decision.justification: Gate results show FR5 and DP5 are well-aligned (FR-DP gate: pass; FR-IM gate: pass) with clear ownership and high verification feasibility. However, DP-IM gate identifies moderate-to-low diffusion risks that require architectural decomposition at the next level to ensure safety-critical consistency guarantees are met. Specifically: (1) incremental update synchronization between topological and geometric layers demands explicit sub-FRs for atomic update semantics and conflict resolution; (2) query interface abstraction requires sub-FRs for schema definition and versioning; (3) consistency monitoring is safety-critical and must be decomposed into dedicated sub-FRs with measurable acceptance criteria. The recommended revisions confirm that DP5 is logically sound but requires refinement through lower-level functional decomposition to address implementation risks. High safety criticality (risk tailoring factor) mandates explicit treatment of consistency, ordering, and fallback behaviors as separate functional concerns rather than implementation details.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Ensure atomic synchronization of topological and geometric layer updates to prevent race conditions and stale-state inconsistencies during incremental sensor integration.
FR5.2: Detect and resolve conflicts between topological and geometric layer representations according to defined authoritativeness rules and reconciliation strategy.

FR5.3: Provide versioned, schema-stable query interfaces for topological (zone/region/access-control) and geometric (obstacle/collision-free-path) abstractions without exposing internal layer representations.
FR5.4: Monitor consistency violations between layers in real-time and escalate safety-critical violations to Safety Services with defined latency and fallback behavior.
FR5.5: Ingest sensor observations (point clouds, zone occupancy, topology updates) with timestamp-based ordering, duplicate detection, and staleness metadata to support downstream query consumers.

revise.why: DP5 is logically complete but lacks explicit specification of: (a) atomic update semantics (versioned snapshots vs. transactional semantics), (b) conflict resolution rules (which layer is authoritative; reconciliation triggers), (c) query interface schema and versioning strategy, (d) consistency violation detection and escalation SLAs, (e) sensor observation ingestion contract (ordering guarantees, staleness windows, batch vs. per-observation semantics). These refinements should be captured as sub-DPs derived from the next-level sub-FRs to ensure implementation risks are mitigated before detailed design.

assumptions: Topological and geometric layer updates can arrive asynchronously from Telemetry without violating safety constraints if atomic synchronization is enforced.
Query consumers (route planning, motion planning) will respect dual-level abstraction boundaries if query interfaces are strictly versioned and documented.
Safety Services can respond to consistency violation escalations within acceptable timeouts (to be defined in FR5.4 acceptance criteria).
Sensor observations include sufficient metadata (timestamps, ordering guarantees) to enable staleness detection and out-of-order handling in NGHE.
Acceptable consistency violation rate and detection latency can be quantified based on worksite operational requirements and safety policies.
open_risks: Moderate: Incremental update synchronization. Asynchronous arrival of topological and geometric updates could cause race conditions or stale-state inconsistencies. Mitigation required at FR5.1 level.

Moderate: Query interface leakage. Tasking consumers may bypass dual-level abstraction and directly access internal representations. Mitigation required at FR5.3 level with strict API contracts and versioning.

Moderate: Conflict resolution authoritativeness. No explicit rule defined for which layer takes precedence in case of conflict (e.g., geometric observation vs. stale topological assumption). Mitigation required at FR5.2 level.

Low: SafetyAlert coupling and escalation timeouts. If Safety Services does not respond quickly to consistency violations, NGHE may accumulate unresolved violations. Mitigation required at FR5.4 level with fallback behaviors.

Low: Sensor observation latency and out-of-order delivery. High-latency or out-of-order observations could produce stale or inconsistent representations. Mitigation required at FR5.5 level with timestamp-based ordering and staleness detection.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of atomic update semantics: versioned snapshots vs. transactional semantics. Include pseudocode or state machine for layer synchronization.

Conflict resolution rules: explicit authoritativeness hierarchy (e.g., geometric observations override topological assumptions) and reconciliation triggers (e.g., geometric update → topological re-inference).

Query interface schema definitions: topological query types (zone adjacency, access control predicates) and geometric query types (nearest-obstacle, collision-free-path). Include versioning and backward compatibility strategy.

Consistency violation detection and escalation SLAs: acceptable violation rate (<1% of updates), detection latency (<100ms), escalation procedure to SafetyAlert, and fallback behavior (e.g., conservative motion constraints).

Sensor observation ingestion contract: expected point cloud density and update frequency, zone occupancy observation format, topology update triggers, timestamp and ordering guarantees, batch vs. per-observation semantics.
Acceptance test plan for FR5.1–FR5.5: measurable criteria for atomic updates, conflict resolution, query interface stability, consistency monitoring, and sensor ingestion robustness.
